coredata.dc:title: Ag-doped TiO2 immobilized cellulose-derived carbon beads: One-Pot preparation, photocatalytic degradation performance and mechanism of ceftriaxone sodium
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jinhui
Luo, Xiaogang
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic degradation is a promising method to solve the problem of ceftriaxone sodium wastewater. In this paper, Ag/TiO2@C composite materials with different Ag molar contents, where cellulose-derived carbon beads used as the carrier, were successfully prepared by the One-Pot method. In Ag/TiO2@C composite photocatalyst, Ag nanoparticles were deposited on TiO2 particles to form a metal-semiconductor structure, where charge carriers were effectively separated under the Surface Plasmon Resonance effect of Ag nanoparticles, thereby extending the lifetime of charge carriers. Ag/TiO2@C has been found of higher photocatalytic degradation performance compared to pure TiO2 and pure carbon beads. Among all, Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C has the best catalytic performance. According to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model, the photodegradation rate followed first-order kinetics, with the highest apparent rate of 0.0091 min−1. According to the results of mass spectrometry, ceftriaxone sodium was degraded into small molecules and eventually degraded into CO2, H2O, and we proposed possible pathways and photocatalytic mechanism accordingly.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic degradation is a promising method to solve the problem of ceftriaxone sodium wastewater. In this paper, Ag/TiO2@C composite materials with different Ag molar contents, where cellulose-derived carbon beads used as the carrier, were successfully prepared by the One-Pot method. In Ag/TiO2@C composite photocatalyst, Ag nanoparticles were deposited on TiO2 particles to form a metal-semiconductor structure, where charge carriers were effectively separated under the Surface Plasmon Resonance effect of Ag nanoparticles, thereby extending the lifetime of charge carriers. Ag/TiO2@C has been found of higher photocatalytic degradation performance compared to pure TiO2 and pure carbon beads. Among all, Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C has the best catalytic performance. According to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model, the photodegradation rate followed first-order kinetics, with the highest apparent rate of 0.0091 min−1. According to the results of mass spectrometry, ceftriaxone sodium was degraded into small molecules and eventually degraded into CO2, H2O, and we proposed possible pathways and photocatalytic mechanism accordingly.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the third-generation cephalosporin’s, ceftriaxone sodium has been widely used in human disease treatment and animal husbandry owing to its broad-spectrum antimicrobial capability [1]. Detection of these residues in wastewater is quite common now. Such residual substances can kill certain microorganisms and propagate the bacteria resistance, endangering biological health and destroying indigenous microbial populations. Thus, reducing the antibiotics’ residual in the environment is very urgent and important [2]. So far, adsorption, photocatalytic oxidation, membrane separation, chemical red-ox, and other technologies have been used for the treatment of antibiotic pollutants.

For a long time, photocatalytic oxidation has been considered as an effective and environmentally friendly technology. In this method, antibiotics are degraded into small molecules including carbon dioxide, water, and other non-toxic compounds [1]. Various materials such as ZnO [3], TiO2 [4], CeO2 [5], BiVO4 [6], MnO2 [7] , and Cu2O [8] have been developed to solve this problem i.e. removing highly toxic pollutants. TiO2 is considered as one of the most potential photocatalysts for the degradation of organic and inorganic pollutants due to its high chemical stability, low cost, and non-toxicity [9]. But, at the same time, a lot of research has been focused on this because of the low quantum yield in TiO2 and the rapid recombination of photo-generated electron and hole pairs. Recent researches show that noble metal deposition, doped transition metals (such as Cu [10], Ag [11], Pd [12,13], Rh [14], Au [15,16], and Ta [17]) or non-metals (such as N [18], S [19], and C [9]) can effectively improve the catalytic site of TiO2. It is noticeable that silver nanoparticles have received widespread attention as important dopants because of their local Surface Plasmon Resonance effect (SPR) [11,20,21]. But, still there are many problems with modified TiO2 in practical applications, such as the recovery and reusability of powder catalysts. Moreover, the possibility of aggregated modified TiO2 particles is large at high concentrations, which is another limiting factor. Yet, whether the TiO2 particles can be immobilized evenly in the carrier is vital for the successful development of TiO2 photocatalysts with good catalytic performance. In recent years, cellulose [22,23], chitosan [19], chitin [13], and g@C3N4 [24–27] have been used to improve the catalytic efficiency of catalyst materials. Cellulose and its derivatives have become the most used adsorbents and carriers because of their easy handling, special structure, and low cost. Carbon support materials were often used as a good adsorbent and photocatalyst carrier [9,28]. In literature [22], it was pointed out that TiO2 was deposited on the surface of cellulose-derived activated carbon using the dipping hydrothermal method. During the process of degrading Methyl orange (MO), it was found that pollutant residues were first adsorbed by carbon and then released to the surface of TiO2 for catalytic degradation.

In this article, our experimental strategy is to deposit Ag nanoparticles on TiO2 surface to form a metal–semiconductor structure and then fix the Ag/TiO2 composite particles on cellulose-derived carbon beads with the One-Pot method to further widen light absorption area and improve the photocatalytic activity. Their photocatalytic activity was tested for higher efficiency degradation of ceftriaxone sodium. During the experiment, we found that the hollow network structure of cellulose-derived carbon beads facilitated the immobilization and uniform distribution of Ag/TiO2 particles and provided a large number of reactive sites. Furthermore, the prepared catalyst has certain stability and reusability, which can provide new insights into photocatalytic models in the fields of environmental protection and wastewater treatment.

Cellulose (cotton linter pulp, α@cellulose% ≥ 95) was provided by Hubei Chemical Fiber Group Ltd (Xiangfan, China). Tetrabutyl titanate and other chemical reagents such as NaOH, urea, H2SO4, HNO3, and AgNO3 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The nanometer titanium dioxide (TiO2, 99.8%, 25 nm, anatase, hydrophilic) was bought from Shanghai McLean biochemical technology Co., Ltd. and the ceftriaxone sodium Trihydrate (C18H16N8Na2O7S3·3.5H2O, ≥98%, M = 661.60) was bought from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. All the materials were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-Pot preparation of Ag-doped TiO2 immobilized carbon beads (Ag/TiO2@C) derived from cellulose
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature [29], Tetrabutyl titanate was used as the precursor for the synthesis of TiO2 in the preparation of Ag-doped TiO2 complexes by the sol–gel method under the supercritical conditions of ethanol. Ag solutions of different molar concentrations were also added to get different concentrations of Ag-doped TiO2 sol. Followed by, the sol was aged for 48 h and dried at 60 °C and the gel was calcined in a tubular temperature-programmed furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and kept at 500 °C for 2 h, then cooled to room temperature, grounded, and passed through a 200-mesh sieve. The final product was an Ag-doped TiO2 composite material coded as Ag/TiO2.

Ag/TiO2 modified cellulose-derived carbon beads were prepared with a One-Pot method. Firstly, cellulose beads were prepared according to the previous works [30–32]. Subsequently, Ag/TiO2 immobilized cellulose beads were heated under a nitrogen and carbon dioxide atmosphere at 600 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace (Carbolite, Modular Horizontal Tube Furnace, GHA 12/600). During that time, the pyrolysis process happened and our Ag/TiO2 immobilized carbon beads (coded as Ag/TiO2@C) were successfully obtained.

Ag/TiO2@C samples were characterized for the morphology and structural properties with the help of field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, GeminiSEM 300, ZEISS). The specific surface area of the materials was determined by using the Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET, Micrometrics ASAP-2020, America) method conducted at liquid-nitrogen temperature (77 K), while pore diameter distribution and pore volume were determined by the Barrett − Joyner − Halenda (BJH) method. The wide-angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on an X-ray diffract meter (Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Germany). The chemical structure and surface chemical state of the samples were observed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo) and Raman spectroscopy (RAMAN, DXR RAMAN MICROSCOPE). The thermal gravimetrical analysis (TGA-DSC, STA449F3) was carried out in temperature range of room temperature to 700 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and the air was used as the carrier gas. The semi-quantitative chemical composition of the sample was examined with X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi+, Thermo) by using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation as the source. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance were carried out on the ultraviolet–visible-near infrared spectrophotometer (DRS, Hitachi UH4150) with BaSO4 as a reflectance standard and it was converted from reflection to absorbance by the Kubelka-Munk method. The Photoluminescence Spectroscopy (PL) was done with an RF-6000 (A40245300201SA) fluorescence spectrophotometer at 380 nm excitation wavelength. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on a CHI660E workstation (Shanghai, China) in a standard three-electrode cell, which contained 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution with a platinum foil and a saturated calomel electrode as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. For the transient photocurrent response density testing, the irradiation was generated from monochromatic light-emitting diodes controlled by a functional generator (Agilent 33210A). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) were recorded with an Electronic paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker EMXplus, Bruker, Germany) at Xe lamp. We used methanol and water as the dispersion system and used DMPO to capture or detect the •O2– radicals and –OH radicals.

For a typical photocatalytic experiment, a 500 W Xenon lamp (CEL-NP 2000, Au light Co. Ltd., China) was used as the light source, and the filter was used to block the light at a wavelength of less than 420 nm. Specifically, a 350 mg photocatalyst was added into ceftriaxone sodium aqueous solution (50 mL, 25 mg L-1) at room temperature and natural pH conditions. The mixture was stirred in darkness (80 min) to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium before light irradiation, and then 2 mL sample was taken from the reactor at different sampling time for analysis. The 2 mL samples were filtrated through a 0.22 μm Polyethersulfone syringe filter, and the corresponding filtrates were collected. The absorbance of the ceftriaxone sodium was recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800) at 254 nm. For comparative study, the photocatalytic degradation of ceftriaxone sodium by pure TiO2 and Ag-free TiO2 immobilized carbon beads was also performed. The photo-degradation rate was calculated by Eq (1). The C0 and C are the chromatographic peak areas of initial time and reaction time, respectively.

The Langmuir − Hinshelwood model (the pseudo-first-order rate equation) was used to illustrate the photodegradation kinetic behavior, shown as follows
(2)

Where C (mg L-1), C0 (mg L-1) are the concentrations of ceftriaxone sodium at different times during the process of degradation and the concentration of adsorption equilibrium, respectively; t (min) is the degradation time and k (min−1) is the apparent rate constant.

The SEM images of pure carbon beads (a, b, c) and Ag/TiO2@C (d, e, f) are shown in Fig. 1 . These carbon beads composite materials exhibited well-defined spherical morphology (Fig. 1a, d). Fig. 1b, c and e, f demonstrated the morphology of the cross-section, showing porous and interpenetrating network structures. The porous network structure formation was due to the phase separation caused by H2O during the sol–gel preparation process not destroyed by Ag/TiO2 particles. Furthermore, it was also discovered that the gas released during the carbonization process had been responsible for the formation of the porous network structure [33]. Similarly, the fact that Ag/TiO2 particles with irregular sizes grew on the surface and inside of the carbon-based material, was evidenced from Fig. 1e, f [34]. Moreover, the surface image of Ag/TiO2@C (Figure S1a) and the cross-sectional images at different magnifications (Figure S1b, c, d) are shown in Figure S1. These porous network structures were conducive to the adsorption of ceftriaxone sodium in aqueous solution, thereby improving the number of reaction sites i.e. quantity of the catalyst.

The element type and content on the Ag/TiO2@C surface were analyzed by EDS patterns (Figure S2 insets). Also, the distribution of Ag/TiO2 on composite beads is characterized by energy dispersive spectroscopy element mapping. As shown in Fig. 1g, Ag/TiO2 particles were uniformly distributed in the carbon-based beads. The results further confirmed the excellent dispensability of TiO2 and Ag nanoparticles and the uniform structure of the composite beads.

To further illustrate the structure of composite material, the specific surface area and the pore diameter distribution of the obtained materials were determined by the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms. From Table S1, the specific surface areas of the pure carbon beads and the composite materials doped with different silver content were 415.237, 214.663, 190.828, 182.278, and 168.452 m2g−1, respectively. With the increased Ag/TiO2 particles attached to the surface and inside the pores, the specific surface area tends to decrease. Figure S3 a, b shows that the pore diameter and pore volume difference between pure carbon beads and composite materials were small [22]. According to the Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller (BDDT) classification, the isotherms can roughly be categorized as type IV, with obvious hysteresis loops observed in the range of 0.85–1.0P/P0. The test results of SEM and BET have confirmed that the composite material had a loose and porous network structure, which produced a large specific surface area and more transmission channels conducive to adsorption and catalysis.

XRD analysis was carried out to investigate the crystal phase of the Ag/TiO2@C composite materials. Fig. 2 A shows the XRD standard card comparison pattern of TiO2 and Ag. Composite materials contained two TiO2 phases (anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 01–0562) and rutile TiO2 (JCPDS No. 01–1292) with different phase ratios. The weak metal diffraction peak of Ag (JCPDS No. 01–1164) was detected in the X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 2A), which confirmed the complete doping of Ag in the TiO2 matrix [35]. Moreover, all XRD data were given in Tables S2, S3, and S4. It was found that a limited amount of Ag ions was incorporated into the TiO2 lattice through the substitution of Ti ions due to the larger size difference between Ti4+ (0.68 Å) and Ag+ (1.26 Å) ions [3,36] confirmed by the XPS test. Fig. 2B shows the XRD patterns of pure TiO2 and Ag/TiO2@C. The XRD patterns indicate that the transformation of anatase to rutile phase in Ag/TiO2@C was promoted with the increase of Ag concentration. This was due to the increased surface defect density of the anatase grain surface, and the rutile phases formed a core, thereby promoting the transformation of the anatase into the rutile phase. The concentration of oxygen vacancies on the anatase grain surface was increased with the increasing Ag concentration, which was beneficial to the ion rearrangement and structural reorganization of the rutile phase [3,37]. The XRD pattern shows that the crystal phase composition in Ag/TiO2@C is a helpful characteristic to be used as a catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) and Raman spectrum
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A shows FT-IR spectrum of pure TiO2 and different Ag/TiO2@C samples, measured in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The materials had a broad absorption peak at 3443.30 cm−1 corresponding to the O-H stretching vibrations of H2O, indicates the existence of absorbed H2O molecules on the surface of catalyst [38], this was also confirmed by subsequent TGA measurement in Fig. 4 a. The absorption peak at 1622.43 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of surface hydroxyl groups (Ti − OH) [39], while, 500–800 cm−1 were put down to the bending vibrations of Ti − O bonds [19]. The small adsorption peak at 1070.85 cm−1 in the spectra of Ag/TiO2@C could be assigned to the vibration of the Ag-Ti-O bond, which further verified the XRD results that Ag was doped into TiO2 lattice. Yet, we noted that all the peaks appeared a slight shift because of doping with Ag, and this effect was a result of the interaction between TiO2 and Ag. Absence of other distinguished peaks related to any other functional groups, except mentioned above, reflected that the synthesized product did not contain any significant impurity [40].

To get more information about pure TiO2 and Ag/TiO2@C, Raman spectra were recorded at room temperature upon excitation with 532 nm laser beams, shown in Fig. 3B. Raman spectrum of anatase TiO2 showed major peaks at 146.15 cm−1, 396.40 cm−1, 518.09 cm−1, and 635.68 cm−1. Raman of all Ag/TiO2@C were also harmonized with Raman active modes of pure TiO2 [41]. A few very small peaks were observed at 614.34 cm−1, 449.18 cm−1, and 143 cm−1, which were characteristic vibration peaks of rutile phase TiO2 [42]. What's more, all Raman shift data for the prepared composites are given in Table S5 and Raman of Ag/TiO2@C confirmed the presence of anatase phase TiO2 and rutile TiO2. It was found that, with the increase of Ag concentration, the Raman spectrum peak intensity decreased slightly. In addition, it could be seen from the Raman spectrum that there were two small and broad peaks corresponding to the D band (1360.69 cm−1) and G band (1587.47 cm−1) of the carbon material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermo-Gravimetric analysis and derivative Thermo-Gravimetric analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The pyrolysis processes of Ag/TiO2@C and pure TiO2 under the air atmosphere were analyzed by TGA. As shown in Fig. 4a, the Ag/TiO2@C composite showed the 3-stage weight loss under the air atmosphere. The first stage was from room temperature to 326.39 °C, resulting in a weight loss of approximately 1.97 wt%, which was due to evaporation of adsorbed H2O molecules and loss of impurities. The second stage (326.39 °C to 481.39 °C) had a larger weight loss of 41.77 wt%, indicating the carbon carrier started to depolymerize and decompose a lot in this temperature range under the air atmosphere. It was observed from the dTGA curve in Fig. 4b that the maximum weight loss rate of the composite material appears at around 441.79 °C [43]. And from the DSC curve in Figure S4b, it could be seen that the first exothermic peak appeared in the composite material at 326.39 °C to 481.39 °C. The third stage from 481.39 °C to 700.00 °C caused a small weight loss of only 1.20 wt%, which was a result of carbon residue degradation, as well as Ag/TiO2 crystal phase transformation, the total mass loss was about 44.94 wt%. The DCS curve of Ag/TiO2@C has a second exothermic peak at 608.89 °C (Figure S4), indicating that the deposited TiO2 has begun to undergo phase transformation [44,45]. So the phase transition temperature of TiO2 in the composite materials was 608.89 °C. The heat transformation process of pure TiO2 under the air atmosphere is shown in Fig. 4a, where the total weight loss was found to be about 5.78 wt%, indicating a part of impurities and water adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 caused weight loss [22].

X-ray photoelectron spectrum was used to determine the presence of Ag at the TiO2 surface as well as to investigate their oxidation states. Fig. 5 a shows the survey of the sample for Ag, Ti, O, C elements. Fig. 5b was the XPS spectrum of C 1 s. The C 1 s peak could be found three peaks with Gaussian-Lorentzian rules at the binding energy of 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 289.8 eV, respectively [46]. The main peak at 284.8 eV corresponded to adventitious carbon and the C-C and C = C bonds. The peak at 286.3 eV attributed to the C-H bond, and the peak at 289.8 eV attributed to the O-C = O bond on the surface of the composite [47], it might be a few C replaced a part of titanium ion in the TiO2 lattice. As shown by the O 1 s in Fig. 5c, the predominant peak of O 1 s (531.8 eV) was put down to the lattice oxygen. The other O 1 s peaks could belong to the O in H2O (533.3 eV) [30,31]. Fig. 5d shows that the Ti 2p photo emission peak of compound material consisted of two peaks, with binding energies of 458.9 eV and 465.2 eV corresponded to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively. This was consistent with the XPS spectrum of TiO2 as described. The peaks at 367.8 eV and 373.8 eV are attributed to Ag0 3d5/2 and Ag0 3d3/2 in Fig. 5e, because the spin energy separation of Ag 3d is 6.0 eV, which is consistent with the standard spin energy separation of Ag, thereby confirming the existence of Ag nanoparticles in Ag/TiO2@C. In addition, it was slightly lower than the standard value (3d5/2, 368.2 eV; 3d3/2, 374.2 eV), indicating that there was an interface interaction between TiO2 and Ag nanoparticles. And we have found that the peaks of Ag+ 3d5/2 and Ag+ 3d3/2 at 366.6 eV and 372.4 eV respectively [20]. This was because there would be a small amount of Ag atoms could substitute the Ti atoms in the TiO2 lattice, forming O-Ag-O bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorption properties and photoelectrical properties

ce:section.ce:para: UV − vis diffuse reflectance (DRS) was conducted to survey the optical photo-absorption and bandgap energies (Eg) of the as-synthesized samples with an integrating sphere of 200–800 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the pure TiO2 and synthetic samples exhibited equal strong absorption in the UV region (below 410 nm), which could be put down to the intrinsic band-band transition from the valence band to the conduction band of TiO2, and this exactly conformed to pure TiO2 light absorption characteristics. Moreover, the Ag/TiO2@C exhibited a typical broad absorption and showed that the absorption of visible light was wider and stronger in the range from 400 to 800 nm, which could be assigned to the Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of metallic species [48–50]. Ag nanoparticles can improve the TiO2 valence band edge that induced visible light absorption and the optical absorption edges of composites were red-shifted, which could have enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the samples under the UV light [3,51,52].

Their band gaps were calculated using Eq (3):
(3)
α h ν = A
h ν -
E g
n / 2

Where α, h, ν, Eg, and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, light frequen, bandgap energy, and a constant, respectively; n was determined by the type of optical transition of a semiconductor (n = 1/2 for direct transition and n = 2 for indirect transition) [53]. The optical transition of TiO2 was indirect. As shown in Fig. 6b, the bandgap energy of pure TiO2 was measured at 3.08 eV, and the Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C was estimated from a plot of (αhv)1/2 as a function of the photon energy (hv) to be approximately 2.21 eV. These values presented were so amusing, and although they may not prove accurately the actual rigid-shift of the valence band (Vb) or the conduction band (Cb) of TiO2, but, they could be explained as a consequence of electronic transitions. For the Ag/TiO2@C materials, since a bit of Ag atoms could substitute Ti atoms in the TiO2 lattice, an O-Ag-O bond was formed, which reduced the electron density, thereby causing the valence band to shift [54,55]. So, it was anticipated that the narrower bandgap of the prepared Ag/TiO2@C composites displayed enhanced photocatalytic performance.

To further confirm the charge transfer behavior of the composite photocatalyst, the PL spectrum of the prepared samples were regarded as the most useful methods for understanding the separation efficiency of photo-generated electrons and holes, and were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Fig. 7 shows the PL spectrum of pure TiO2 and Ag/TiO2@C composite catalysts. It could be seen from the PL spectrum that pure TiO2 had the highest emission intensity at 457 nm, as it formed a complex with carbon beads and Ag, its emission intensity first decreased and then had an increasing tendency as the Ag content increased [9]. When the composite materials were doped with a small amount of Ag nanoparticles, it could act as a good electron acceptor and could capture electrons (e-) on the TiO2 surface, weakening the recombination of photogenerated electrons (e-) and holes (h+) [35]. But, excessive doping of Ag might form photogenerated electrons-empty at the recombination center of the pair, the electron and hole transport to the surface of TiO2 was hindered, which in turn reduced the photocatalytic efficiency. As per these results Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C exhibited the best activity, which was consistent with the transient photocurrent response results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and photocurrent responses
ce:section.ce:para: The arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot can embody the separation process of electrons-holes pairs and the interfacial charge migration. It has been believed that the smaller the radius of the arc, the lower the resistance of the interface charges transfer, and the higher the efficiency of charge transfer and separation. The EIS data were measured by the three-electrode system, and the Nyquist plot could be explained by an equivalent circuit, as shown in Figure S8. We have measured the EIS of pure TiO2 and Ag/TiO2@C (Fig. 8 A) to further verify the interface generation and effective separation of photogenerated carriers in the sample. As shown in Fig. 8C, the Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C sample displayed the smallest arc radius in the EIS Nyquist plot compared to pure TiO2 and other prepared samples, which indicates that Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C had a lower interface resistance. Moreover, the conductivity of the prepared material obviously decreased and the charge transfer resistances significantly increased with the Ag concentration increase, these effects led to the decrease in the activity of the catalyst [56]. It was shown that the modification of TiO2 with Ag nanoparticles and carbon materials could have improved the conductivity and accelerated the charge transfer, which were the key factors in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalyst [49].

The photocurrent density-time (i-t) curve is a common method to study the separation efficiency of electron holes [57]. Maintaining identical operational conditions, the higher photocurrent intensity implies more efficient electron − hole pairs’ separation. The transient photocurrent responses of pure TiO2 and Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C composites under visible light (λ > 420 nm) were recorded and plotted in Fig. 8B. As shown in Fig. 8B, when the prepared working electrode was exposed to visible light, both two samples exhibited a fast response, but the emerged transient photocurrent was different. For pure TiO2 samples, the photocurrent response to visible light was weakened. This was due to the presence of oxygen vacancies in the TiO2 particles, which might lead to the rapid combination of electron-hole pairs and it has also been reported in many previous papers [58]. Besides, we noticed that Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C exhibited high photocurrent density indicating that the photoresponse ability of the composite material and the separation of electron-hole pairs were improved [27,28]. This result was consistent with the results of the DRS spectrum, PL spectrum, and EIS spectrum.

The EPR measurements were conducted to confirm the reactive oxygen species evolved in the photocatalysis over composite materials with 5,5-dimethyl-1pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in methanol and aqueous dispersion [14]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, a weak •O2– signal could be observed in the darkness under identical conditions. With the time increased, the main peak increased and new four distinct peak signals with Ag/TiO2@C in methanol were generated, which could be distributed to DMPO−•O2– under light illumination [24]. The results confirmed that •O2− was the main reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation process [59,60]. So, it was concluded from Fig. 9b that the peak signals of •OH were weakened or even disappeared with the time, which indicated that the •OH was not the main active species in the photocatalytic process. These results indicate that the amount of •OH radicals that participated in the photocatalytic reaction was small, which is consistent with the previous report [37]. Thus, the radical trapping experiments confirmed the important role of •OH and •O2− in the photocatalytic degradation process [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of ceftriaxone sodium and regeneration experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic performance of the synthesized composite catalyst was evaluated by degrading the ceftriaxone sodium solution under simulated visible light (500 W Xe lamp) irradiation. As shown in Figure S5, adsorption experiments of Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C were performed in darkness. Composite materials had an efficient adsorption performance, and the adsorption rate reached 37.36%. The adsorption equilibrium was achieved at around 80 min for the samples. The adsorption results of the catalyst material were attributed to the certain adsorption capacity of carbon beads. Before starting the xenon lamp, the catalytic system was left under the dark for 80 min to reach the adsorption equilibrium to further investigate the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the degradation efficiency of catalyst-free system was only 3.168% under simulated visible light irradiation, which meant that there was a little photodegradation of ceftriaxone sodium solution. Ag/TiO2@C with Ag (0.5%) doping, pure TiO2 powder, TiO2@C powder and Ag/TiO2@C with 0.75% Ag doping showed the maximum degradation efficiency of ceftriaxone sodium at 91.92%, 54.56%, 62.7% and 77.42% after 270 min, respectively. Its photocatalytic efficiency dropped dramatically as the Ag concentration continued to increase, because when the Ag doping amount was too high, metal ions itself might form a composite center of electron-hole pairs, consequently reduced the photocatalytic efficiency. Fig. 10b is the temporal evolution of the UV − vis absorption of ceftriaxone sodium, and most of the ceftriaxone sodium was removed after 270 min irradiation. The pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics was fitted according to the Eq (1). The k values of different materials were calculated from the data given in Fig. 10c. The corresponding k and fitting coefficient (R2) for composite materials were listed in Table S6. The largest k belongs to Ag/TiO2@C prepared by doping Ag (0.50%). But, the reaction rate constant k decreased with the continuous doping of Ag. The high fitting coefficient of measurement (R2 > 0.9) indicates that the catalytic reaction well-fitted to the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics. The photocatalytic efficiency of the sample reduced by 17.96% after 5 cycles (Fig. 10d). It shows that Ag/TiO2@C has certain stability and the catalytic efficiency reduction is due to the loss in the recovery process.

The relationship between ceftriaxone sodium concentration and degradation efficiency was studied and represented in Figure S6. 350 mg Ag (0.5%) /TiO2@C was used to degrade ceftriaxone sodium at different concentrations. As the concentration of ceftriaxone sodium increases, the degradation efficiency increased at first and decreased afterward. Under the same experimental conditions, the active sites of the catalyst and the active species formed on the surface of the catalyst had been constant. At higher concentrations of ceftriaxone sodium, it would have been adsorbed on the inside and surface of the catalyst lead to covering of the active sites and ultimately the formation of reactive active species decreased, thereby reducing the degradation efficiency. Thus, under these experimental conditions, the optimal concentration of ceftriaxone sodium was 25 mg L-1. In Figure S7, we studied the relationship between the pH value of the ceftriaxone sodium solution and the degradation efficiency also. We can see that as the pH value of ceftriaxone sodium solution increased, the degradation efficiency first increased and then decreased. When the pH was around 8, the highest value was 83.29%. This was consistent with the experimental results obtained previously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of ceftriaxone sodium
ce:section.ce:para:
It is well known that the photocatalytic process is an interfacial process, and the good dispersion of doped transition metal nanoparticles on the semiconductor surface promotes charge carriers separation, thereby enhances catalytic performance. Thus, the corresponding mechanism is proposed in Fig. 11 . Photocatalytic reactions were initiated by irradiating the catalyst surface with simulated sunlight, thereby generating photo-excited electron and hole pairs (Eq (4)).
(4) Ag/TiO2@C + hʋ→Ag/TiO2@C (h+ (Vb) + e− (Cb))

Then, the dopant metal nanoparticles can serve as important capture centers and can separately trap photo-induced (e-)/(h+). Since metal nanoparticles are effective receivers of electrons, and the scramble for electrons by metal nanoparticles reduces the recombination of photo-generated electrons (e-) and photo-generated holes (h+) on the surface of TiO2 so that the surface of TiO2 under the action of light radiation can produce more •OH and •O2−, which improves the catalytic activity (Eq (5) and Eq (6)).
(5) Ag0 + h+ (Vb) → Ag+
(6) Ag++e- (Cb) → Ag0

According to literature reports [22], carbon beads can also serve as an electron reservoir, which can capture electrons emitted from the conduction band of TiO2, thereby improving the separation efficiency of charge carriers. The photo-electron can reduce O2 to produce superoxide radicals anions •O2− (Eq (7)). It further reacts with water molecules to generate hydroperoxy radicals (•OOH) and hydroxide ions (OH−) (Eq (8)) leading to the generation of oxidative H2O2 molecule (Eq (9)). At the same time, the hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are formed by the reaction of positive holes with H2O or OH– (Eq 10, Eq (11)). The generated oxidative substances during this process, such as •O2 −, H2O2, h+ and •OH would oxidize ceftriaxone sodium (cs) and break it down into small molecules i.e. carbon dioxide and water (Eq (12)).
(7) O2 + e- (Cb) →•O2 −
(8) •O2−+H2O→•OOH + OH−
(9) 2•OOH → H2O2 + O2
(11) H2O + h+ (Vb) → •OH + H+ (10) OH−+h+ (Vb) → •OH
(12) •O2 −+•OH + H2O2 + h+ (Vb) + cs → Intermediates + H2O + CO2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of degradation intermediates, final products, and possible degradation pathway
ce:section.ce:para: The possible degraded intermediates and products were identified by using the MS (see Figure S9). Scheme 1 illustrates the possible degradation pathways. A protonated ion peak at m/z 598.59 appeared corresponding to the ceftriaxone sodium after losing 3.5 H2O molecules. Due to the carboxylation of ceftriaxone sodium, a molecular ion peak appeared at m/z 576.59. The molecular ion peak decreased and appearance of some extra ion peaks with the radiation time indicate that the antibiotic was being degraded. The fragments at m/z 413.90, m/z 530.59, and m/z 396.03 might be appeared due to the C-N bond rupture from the C-8 side chain, loss of carbonyl group, C-S bond rupture, and adjacent position rearrangement, respectively. B (m/z 413.90) was degraded to E (m/z 159.00) and could further fragment into I (m/z 127.00), CO2, and H2O with the progress of the degradation process. The C (m/z 576.59) can degrade to F (m/z 412.01) due to C-S bond rupture, the formation of J (m/z 243.04) can be attributed to the fracture of β-lactam from the intermediate C (m/z 576.59), afterward, J could further fragment into L (m/z 112.98) because of the C-C bond rupture from 5, 6 position carbons. The formation of K (m/z 282.00) could be attributed to the fracture of β-lactam from the intermediate G (m/z 530.59) and could further decompose to G (m/z 181.00) with C-S bond rupture from the C-3 chain. D (m/z 396.03) has decomposed to H (m/z 112.98), CO2, and H2O. Finally, all these intermediate products can be degraded to CO2, H2O, and other small molecular substances.

In summary, Ag-doped TiO2 immobilized cellulose-derived carbon beads (Ag/TiO2@C) were synthesized through One-Pot method, which were innovatively used as a photocatalyst to treat ceftriaxone sodium residue in wastewater. The morphology and structure of Ag/TiO2@C, elemental composition and optical properties were characterized by SEM, EDS, BET, XRD, Raman, XPS, UV-DRS, PL spectroscopy, and electron paramagnetic resonance technology. It was observed that Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C had better catalytic performance for the degradation of ceftriaxone sodium under simulated sunlight. In this Ag/TiO2@C photocatalyst, TiO2 served as the main light absorber, and under the Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of the Ag nanoparticles, charge carriers were effectively separated, thereby enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency. Ag/TiO2@C had better stability in the photocatalytic process owing to their specific structure. We proposed possible pathways and photocatalytic mechanism. Besides, Ag/TiO2@C materials can be recycled and reused. The current work may provide new insights into building novel photocatalysts and may give a promising way for environmental remediation.

The SEM images and EDS patterns of Ag/TiO2@C (Figure S1, S2). The pore size distribution of the pure carbon beads and composite materials (Figure S3, Table S1). The TGA curves and the DCS curves of pure TiO2, Ag (0.50%)/TiO2@C under air atmosphere (Figure S4). The adsorption rate curves of the Ag (0.5%)/TiO2@C for ceftriaxone sodium solution (Figure S5). The effect of initial concentration of ceftriaxone sodium on degradation efficiency (Figure S6). The effect of pH values of ceftriaxone sodium solution on degradation efficiency (Figure S7). The circuit diagram during EIS test analysis (Figure S8). The m/z of ceftriaxone sodium degradation with samples. (Figure S9). The X-ray diffraction peaks test data (Table S2, S3, S4). The Raman spectroscopy test data (Table S5). Reaction rate constant (k) and R2 for photocatalytic degradation of ceftriaxone sodium (Table S6).
